calendar.matters:
CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Feb-26-2024 Off Calendar

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Reconsideration Of Order Granting Defendants' Motion To Transfer Venue
Off Calendar Jan-22-2024 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF MELISSA WILLIAMSON's Motion For Reconsideration Of Order Granting Defendants' Motion To Transfer Venue.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff's motion for reconsideration is denied. First, caselaw provides that plaintiff's exclusive remedy for relief from a venue order is to seek a writ of mandate per CCP 400. (Dunas v. Superior Court (1970) 9 Cal.App.3d 236, 239-240 [court lacked jurisdiction to hear motion for reconsideration of an order granting motion to change venue]; Game Stop, Inc. v. Superior Court (2018) 26 Cal.App.5th 502, 511["A party aggrieved by an order granting or denying a motion to change the place of trial must seek review by way of a petition for writ of mandate." (emphasis added)]; but see Graham v. Hansen (1982) 128 Cal.App.3d 965, 970 [noting that Dunas held that there was no jurisdiction to entertain a motion of reconsideration after a ruling on a change of venue motion because there was no statutory authority for the motion at the time and the Legislature subsequently amended CCP 1008].)

Second, even if the court could entertain this motion, it fails on the merits. A party seeking reconsideration must demonstrate diligence. (Baldwin v. Home Sav. Of America (1997) 59 Cal.App.4th 1192, 1200.) The court previously concluded that CCP 395(b) controlled the venue determination. Venue would be proper in San Francisco if plaintiff resided in San Francisco at the time the parties contracted. Based on the record before it, the court reasonably inferred that plaintiff did not reside in San Francisco and defendant demonstrated that there was no other basis for San Francisco venue. At the hearing, plaintiff did not argue that she resided in San Francisco at the time of the contracts. Rather, she stressed that some of the property stored was "business property." In this motion for reconsideration, plaintiff now asserts that she resided in San Francisco when the contracts were executed. Plaintiff was not diligent as she easily could have clarified her address in her opposition or at the hearing after defendants made a prima facie showing that plaintiff did not reside in San Francisco at the time of the contracts. This motion is denied due to the lack of diligence. Plaintiff recycled arguments regarding "business property" are also not good cause to grant reconsideration.

Defendants' request for sanctions per CCP 1008(d) is denied.

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: S. Kane; Not Reported. (302/RBU)
